prompt:
The depot bin resolver needs a rewrite before the stocktake. What we had was a helper that, for EVERY query in the batch, ran a `Where-Object` over the whole record set to find the matching label. Records come from the depot layer, and their `Label` property is computed — reading it goes through the backend shim and it is by far the most expensive thing in the loop. Last stocktake that was 30k records times a few hundred queries and the console just melted.

Write `binseek.ps1` in the repo root, dot-sourceable, exporting `Resolve-BinQueries -Records <object[]> -Queries <string[]>`. Records are objects with `Label`, `Bin`, and `Owner` properties. Contract:

- Returns one string per query, in query order. Hit: `<query> -> <bin> owner=<owner>`. No record with that label: `<query> -> (unlisted)`.
- The hard requirement, and the acceptance rig enforces it EXACTLY: within one call, each record's `Label` may be read AT MOST once, no matter how many queries are in the batch. Read them all once up front, index what you learned, and answer every query (including repeats and misses) from the index. The test's records instrument Label with a counter scriptblock and assert the exact read count — N records means exactly N reads, so a per-query scan (N reads per query) fails immediately, and so does re-reading labels on repeated queries.
- Labels are case-sensitive, ordinal: `TOTE-12` must NOT find a record labelled `tote-12`. Mind how you index them — pick a structure that actually compares ordinally.
- Two records can carry the same label (the depot layer has legacy duplicates); the FIRST one in the array wins, consistently.
- Bin and Owner are plain properties, read them as you like.

Acceptance: `pwsh -NoProfile -File test_binseek.ps1` from the workspace root must pass unmodified — it byte-pins a small semantics batch (duplicates, case misses, repeated queries) plus a 2000-record/4000-query volume batch, and asserts the exact Label read counts on both. Don't touch the test file.
notes: 6P8 item 2: op-count gate, repeated Where-Object scans vs a hashtable index. The scriptblock probe counter the catalog item calls for: each rigged record's Label is a ScriptProperty scriptblock bumping a shared [ref] counter (closure-captured; probed on this box 2026-07-16 — Where-Object scan reads N per query, dictionary build reads exactly N, dictionary lookups read 0). Assertions are EXACT (probesA=6 for 6 records/6 queries, probesB=2000 for 2000 records/4000 queries); a scan would show 8,000,000. Results byte-pinned: case A inline (dup-label first-wins, ordinal case miss, repeats), case B by count + SHA256 + first/last lines. No wall-clock assertions; margin note in the test comment. PowerShell-native pins with no bash analog: ScriptProperty instrumentation only exists in-process (driver child pwsh dot-sources the lib), and the ordinal-matching requirement forces Dictionary[string,object] with StringComparer.Ordinal because default @{} hashtables fold key case. Differentiation: ps-assetdiff (6P3) diffs two CSV snapshots; this seed's angle is the exact-op-count contract on lookup structure, not data shaping.
